1. sleep2vec: Unified Cross-Modal Alignment for Heterogeneous Nocturnal Biosignals (arXiv.org 2026, cites: 0)
   Tasks ranging from sleep staging to clinical diagnosis traditionally rely on standard polysomnography (PSG) devices, bedside monitors and wearable devices, which capture diverse nocturnal biosignals (e.g., EEG, EOG, ECG, SpO$_2$). However, heterogeneity across devices and frequent sensor dropout pose significant challenges for unified modelling of these multimodal signals. We present \texttt{sleep2vec}, a foundation model for diverse and incomplete nocturnal biosignals that learns a shared representation via cross-modal alignment. \texttt{sleep2vec} is contrastively pre-trained on 42,249 overnight recordings spanning nine modalities using a \textit{Demography, Age, Site \&History-aware InfoNCE} objective that incorporates physiological and acquisition metadata (\textit{e.g.}, age, gender, recording site) to dynamically weight negatives and mitigate cohort-specific shortcuts. On downstream sleep staging and clinical outcome assessment, \texttt{sleep2vec} consistently outperforms strong baselines and remains robust to any subset of available modalities and sensor dropout. We further characterize, to our knowledge for the first time, scaling laws for nocturnal biosignals with respect to modality diversity and model capacity. Together, these results show that unified cross-modal alignment, coupled with principled scaling, enables label-efficient, general-purpose modelling of real-world nocturnal biosignals.

2. Alignment-Aware and Reliability-Gated Multimodal Fusion for Unmanned Aerial Vehicle Detection Across Heterogeneous Thermal-Visual Sensors ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Reliable unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) detection is critical for autonomous airspace monitoring but remains challenging when integrating sensor streams that differ substantially in resolution, perspective, and field of view. Conventional fusion methods-such as wavelet-, Laplacian-, and decision-level approaches-often fail to preserve spatial correspondence across modalities and suffer from annotation of inconsistencies, limiting their robustness in real-world settings. This study introduces two fusion strategies, Registration-aware Guided Image Fusion (RGIF) and Reliability-Gated Modality-Attention Fusion (RGMAF), designed to overcome these limitations. RGIF employs Enhanced Correlation Coefficient (ECC)-based affine registration combined with guided filtering to maintain thermal saliency while enhancing structural detail. RGMAF integrates affine and optical-flow registration with a reliability-weighted attention mechanism that adaptively balances thermal contrast and visual sharpness. Experiments were conducted on the Multi-Sensor and Multi-View Fixed-Wing (MMFW)-UAV dataset comprising 147,417 annotated air-to-air frames collected from infrared, wide-angle, and zoom sensors. Among single-modality detectors, YOLOv10x demonstrated the most stable cross-domain performance and was selected as the detection backbone for evaluating fused imagery. RGIF improved the visual baseline by 2.13% mAP@50 (achieving 97.65%), while RGMAF attained the highest recall of 98.64%. These findings show that registration-aware and reliability-adaptive fusion provides a robust framework for integrating heterogeneous modalities, substantially enhancing UAV detection performance in multimodal environments.

3. RCMCL: A Unified Contrastive Learning Framework for Robust Multi-Modal (RGB-D, Skeleton, Point Cloud) Action Understanding ( 2025, cites: 0)
   Human action recognition (HAR) with multi-modal inputs (RGB-D, skeleton, point cloud) can achieve high accuracy but typically relies on large labeled datasets and degrades sharply when sensors fail or are noisy. We present Robust Cross-Modal Contrastive Learning (RCMCL), a self-supervised framework that learns modality-invariant representations and remains reliable under modality dropout and corruption. RCMCL jointly optimizes (i) a cross-modal contrastive objective that aligns heterogeneous streams, (ii) an intra-modal self-distillation objective that improves view-invariance and reduces redundancy, and (iii) a degradation simulation objective that explicitly trains models to recover from masked or corrupted inputs. At inference, an Adaptive Modality Gating (AMG) network assigns data-driven reliability weights to each modality for robust fusion. On NTU RGB+D 120 (CS/CV) and UWA3D-II, RCMCL attains state-of-the-art accuracy in standard settings and exhibits markedly better robustness: under severe dual-modality dropout it shows only an 11.5% degradation, significantly outperforming strong supervised fusion baselines. These results indicate that self-supervised cross-modal alignment, coupled with explicit degradation modeling and adaptive fusion, is key to deployable multi-modal HAR.

4. A Unified Self-Supervised Framework for Scalable Analytics on Heterogeneous Unstructured Data (2025 International Conference on Electrical, Electronics, and Computer Science with Advance Power Technologies - A Future Trends (ICE2CPT) 2025, cites: 0)
   Unstructured data—ranging from text and images to audio and sensor streams—constitutes the majority of global digital content but remains largely untapped due to annotation scarcity and heterogeneity. In this paper, we present a unified self-supervised learning (SSL) framework tailored to analytics on unstructured data. Our approach integrates masked content prediction, contrastive learning, and cross-modal correlation objectives within a single architecture, enabling it to learn rich and transferable representations from raw, unlabeled inputs. Unlike modality-specific methods, our framework general- izes seamlessly across text, vision, and multimodal domains, making it particularly suitable for real-world deployment. Experiments on benchmark datasets—including ImageNet, AG News, and MS-COCO—demonstrate state-of-the-art performance in classification and retrieval tasks, without relying on human supervision. Our model's robustness, adaptability, and cross-domain transferability position it as a practical foundation for next-generation unstructured data analytics.

5. PhysioBridge: Physiology-Constrained Self-Supervised Foundation Model for Cross-Device ECG–PPG Learning with Conformal Risk Control (Proceedings of the 2026 2nd International Conference on Computing and Emerging Sciences 2026, cites: 0)
   Wearable and bedside sensors continuously generate electrocardiograms (ECG), photoplethysmograms (PPG), and related physiological waveforms that could enable earlier detection of deterioration and more personalized care. However, current deep learning pipelines in biomedical signal processing often remain task- and device-specific, degrade under domain shift (new hospitals, sensors, skin tones, motion), and provide limited uncertainty information for safety-critical decisions. We propose PhysioBridge, a foundation-model approach that learns a shared representation space for ECG and PPG via self-supervised pretraining and explicit physiology constraints, then supports downstream adaptation with distribution-free risk control. PhysioBridge introduces (i) multi-rate patch tokenization that preserves clinically meaningful morphology across heterogeneous sampling rates; (ii) coupled masked modeling and cross-modal contrastive alignment to bind ECG and PPG dynamics while remaining label-efficient; (iii) differentiable physiology regularizers that enforce heart-rate consistency and pulse-transit-time plausibility; and (iv) calibrated conformal prediction wrappers that provide coverage guarantees for classification and regression under minimal assumptions. We specify a reproducible evaluation protocol on public waveform resources and ECG corpora, emphasizing cross-device generalization, out-of-distribution detection, and fairness-aware reporting. PhysioBridge is positioned as a practical step toward trustworthy AI foundations for healthcare signal intelligence—supporting scalable reuse, transparent risk management, and safer deployment pathways.

6. GeoMeld: Toward Semantically Grounded Foundation Models for Remote Sensing ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Effective foundation modeling in remote sensing requires spatially aligned heterogeneous modalities coupled with semantically grounded supervision, yet such resources remain limited at scale. We present GeoMeld, a large-scale multimodal dataset with approximately 2.5 million spatially aligned samples. The dataset spans diverse modalities and resolutions and is constructed under a unified alignment protocol for modality-aware representation learning. GeoMeld provides semantically grounded language supervision through an agentic captioning framework that synthesizes and verifies annotations from spectral signals, terrain statistics, and structured geographic metadata, encoding measurable cross-modality relationships within textual descriptions. To leverage this dataset, we introduce GeoMeld-FM, a pretraining framework that combines multi-pretext masked autoencoding over aligned modalities, JEPA representation learning, and caption-vision contrastive alignment. This joint objective enables the learned representation space to capture both reliable cross-sensor physical consistency and grounded semantics. Experiments demonstrate consistent gains in downstream transfer and cross-sensor robustness. Together, GeoMeld and GeoMeld-FM establish a scalable reference framework for semantically grounded multi-modal foundation modeling in remote sensing.

7. SMARTIES: Spectrum-Aware Multi-Sensor Auto-Encoder for Remote Sensing Images (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 14)
   From optical sensors to microwave radars, leveraging the complementary strengths of remote sensing (RS) sensors is crucial for achieving dense spatio-temporal monitoring of our planet. In contrast, recent deep learning models, whether task-specific or foundational, are often specific to single sensors or to fixed combinations: adapting such models to different sensory inputs requires both architectural changes and re-training, limiting scalability and generalization across multiple RS sensors. On the contrary, a single model able to modulate its feature representations to accept diverse sensors as input would pave the way to agile and flexible multi-sensor RS data processing. To address this, we introduce SMARTIES, a generic and versatile foundation model lifting sensor-specific/dependent efforts and enabling scalability and generalization to diverse RS sensors: SMARTIES projects data from heterogeneous sensors into a shared spectrum-aware space, enabling the use of arbitrary combinations of bands both for training and inference. To obtain sensor-agnostic representations, we train a single, unified transformer model reconstructing masked multi-sensor data with cross-sensor token mixup. On both single- and multi-modal tasks across diverse sensors, SMARTIES outperforms previous models that rely on sensor-specific pretraining. Our code and pretrained models are available at https://gsumbul.github.io/SMARTIES.

8. Promoting cross-modal representations to improve multimodal foundation models for physiological signals (arXiv.org 2024, cites: 15)
   Many healthcare applications are inherently multimodal, involving several physiological signals. As sensors for these signals become more common, improving machine learning methods for multimodal healthcare data is crucial. Pretraining foundation models is a promising avenue for success. However, methods for developing foundation models in healthcare are still in early exploration and it is unclear which pretraining strategies are most effective given the diversity of physiological signals. This is partly due to challenges in multimodal health data: obtaining data across many patients is difficult and costly, there is a lot of inter-subject variability, and modalities are often heterogeneously informative across downstream tasks. Here, we explore these challenges in the PhysioNet 2018 dataset. We use a masked autoencoding objective to pretrain a multimodal model. We show that the model learns representations that can be linearly probed for a diverse set of downstream tasks. We hypothesize that cross-modal reconstruction objectives are important for successful multimodal training, as they encourage the model to integrate information across modalities. We demonstrate that modality dropout in the input space improves performance across downstream tasks. We also find that late-fusion models pretrained with contrastive learning objectives are less effective across multiple tasks. Finally, we analyze the model's representations, showing that attention weights become more cross-modal and temporally aligned with our pretraining strategy. The learned embeddings also become more distributed in terms of the modalities encoded by each unit. Overall, our work demonstrates the utility of multimodal foundation models with health data, even across diverse physiological data sources. We further argue that explicit methods for inducing cross-modality may enhance multimodal pretraining strategies.

9. Multimodal Machine Learning Models for Intelligent Interpretation of Text, Image and Audio Inputs (2025 5th International Conference on Emerging Research in Electronics, Computer Science and Technology (ICERECT) 2025, cites: 0)
   Multimodal learning architectures have emerged as a critical paradigm in artificial intelligence (AI), enabling systems to process and integrate heterogeneous data modalities such as text, vision, audio, and sensor inputs for comprehensive understanding and decision-making. Traditional unimodal models often fail to capture the rich, complementary information available across different modalities, leading to suboptimal performance in complex real-world tasks. This paper explores the design and development of multimodal learning architectures that effectively fuse features from diverse modalities through early, late, and hybrid fusion strategies, along with attention-based mechanisms and crossmodal transformers. Recent advancements, including contrastive learning and large-scale pretraining on multimodal datasets, have significantly improved the performance of applications such as visual question answering, speech recognition, sentiment analysis, and autonomous systems. The proposed framework emphasizes modality alignment, feature representation learning, and robust fusion techniques to address challenges such as data heterogeneity, modality imbalance, and missing modalities. Moreover, the role of selfsupervised learning and generative models, including multimodal large language models (MLLMs), is discussed in enhancing cross-modal understanding and zero-shot generalization. We highlight benchmark datasets, evaluation metrics, and case studies that demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed approaches. Finally, open challenges such as interpretability, computational efficiency, and ethical considerations are outlined, with future research directions aimed at building scalable, generalizable, and human-centric multimodal systems.

10. AviationLMM: A Large Multimodal Foundation Model for Civil Aviation (2025 5th International Conference on Information Communication and Software Engineering (ICICSE) 2025, cites: 0)
   Civil aviation is a cornerstone of global transportation and commerce, and ensuring its safety, efficiency and customer satisfaction is paramount. Yet conventional Artificial Intelligence (AI) solutions in aviation remain siloed and narrow, focusing on isolated tasks or single modalities. They struggle to integrate heterogeneous data such as voice communications, radar tracks, sensor streams and textual reports, which limits situational awareness, adaptability, and real- time decision support. This paper introduces the vision of AviationLMM, a Large Multimodal foundation Model for civil aviation, designed to unify the heterogeneous data streams of civil aviation and enable understanding, reasoning, generation and agentic applications. We firstly identify the gaps between existing AI solutions and requirements. Secondly, we describe the model architecture that ingests multimodal inputs such as air-ground voice, surveillance, on-board telemetry, video and structured texts, and performs cross-modal alignment and fusion, and produces flexible outputs ranging from situation summaries and risk alerts to predictive diagnostics and multimodal incident reconstructions. In order to fully realize this vision, we identify key research opportunities to address, including data acquisition, alignment and fusion, pretraining, reasoning, trustworthiness, privacy, robustness to missing modalities, and synthetic scenario generation. By articulating the design and challenges of AviationLMM, we aim to boost the civil aviation foundation model progress and catalyze coordinated research efforts toward an integrated, trustworthy and privacy - preserving aviation AI ecosystem.

1. Heterogeneous Graph Masked Autoencoders (AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence 2022, cites: 121)
   Generative self-supervised learning (SSL), especially masked autoencoders, has become one of the most exciting learning paradigms and has shown great potential in handling graph data. However, real-world graphs are always heterogeneous, which poses three critical challenges that existing methods ignore: 1) how to capture complex graph structure? 2) how to incorporate various node attributes? and 3) how to encode different node positions? In light of this, we study the problem of generative SSL on heterogeneous graphs and propose HGMAE, a novel heterogeneous graph masked autoencoder model to address these challenges. HGMAE captures comprehensive graph information via two innovative masking techniques and three unique training strategies. In particular, we first develop metapath masking and adaptive attribute masking with dynamic mask rate to enable effective and stable learning on heterogeneous graphs. We then design several training strategies including metapath-based edge reconstruction to adopt complex structural information, target attribute restoration to incorporate various node attributes, and positional feature prediction to encode node positional information. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HGMAE outperforms both contrastive and generative state-of-the-art baselines on several tasks across multiple datasets. Codes are available at https://github.com/meettyj/HGMAE.

2. MaskPOI: A POI Representation Learning Method Using Graph Mask Modeling (Electronics 2025, cites: 1)
   Point of Interest (POI) data play a critical role in enabling location-based services (LBS) by providing intrinsic attributes, including geographic coordinates and semantic categories, alongside a spatial context that reflects relationships among POIs. However, the inherent label sparsity in POI datasets poses significant challenges for traditional supervised learning approaches. To address this limitation, we propose MaskPOI, a novel self-supervised learning framework that combines the strengths of graph neural networks and masked modeling. MaskPOI incorporates two complementary modules: an edge mask-based graph autoencoder that models the spatial topology by predicting edge existence and uncovering hidden spatial relationships and a feature mask-based graph autoencoder that reconstructs masked node features to explore the rich attribute characteristics of POIs. Together, these modules enable MaskPOI to jointly capture the spatial and attribute information essential for robust representation learning. Extensive experiments demonstrate MaskPOI’s effectiveness in improving performance on downstream tasks such as functional zone classification and population density prediction. Ablation studies further validate the contributions of its components, highlighting MaskPOI as a powerful and versatile framework for POI representation learning.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Masked-prediction pretraining transformed language and vision;
how can we adapt the same principle to other data types like
graphs, time series, or sensor streams, and what objective
design choices matter?


### Proposed method
We propose a unified masked-prediction framework for non-text, non-image data called Structure- and Alignment-aware Masked Modeling (SAMM). SAMM treats graphs, time series, and heterogeneous sensor streams as token sequences enriched with structure. Tokenization is modality-specific but follows a common interface: each token carries a value embedding, a position/structure embedding, and a type embedding. For time series and sensor streams, we form variable-length temporal patches via change-point detection or fixed windows (with overlap) and optionally add frequency-domain tokens from short-time Fourier or wavelet transforms. For graphs, both nodes and edges become tokens, and attention is biased by graph distances and edge types; we also allow subgraph tokens produced by a lightweight pooling operator. Tokens from all sources are projected with shallow, modality-specific linear layers into a single fusion transformer, avoiding heavy encoders while preserving modality identity via type and structure embeddings.

Masking is a mixture of random, structure-aware, and importance-aware policies. In streams, we mask contiguous spans, full channels, and frequency bands; in graphs, we mask nodes, edges, and coherent subgraphs (communities or k-hop balls). To make masking informative rather than arbitrary, we maintain a fast rationale scorer that estimates per-token importance from current representations; we sample a fixed budget of hard masks from high-importance tokens and fill the remainder with random or structure-contiguous masks to cover both critical and background context. We also apply modality dropout at sequence level (e.g., blank all tactile tokens while keeping vision) to force cross-sensor prediction.

Prediction targets are chosen to avoid regression-to-the-mean and to match measurement noise. For continuous values, we predict discrete latent codes produced by a small vector-quantizer trained jointly with dequantization noise, plus an auxiliary residual head for fine error correction. Stream decoders predict both time-domain tokens and spectro-temporal tokens to capture local dynamics and periodicity; graph decoders predict masked node/edge attributes and edge existence. We optimize a multitask objective: masked-token cross-entropy on codes and L1/L2 on residuals; a cross-sensor contrastive alignment that brings co-temporal segments from different modalities together and pushes negatives apart; a pairwise matching loss that discriminates correctly synchronized pairs from time-shifted or modality-mismatched pairs; and a temporal-ordering loss on shuffled spans. For paired modalities with expected near-monotonic timing (e.g., audio–text, audio–vibration), we compute a soft alignment path from cross-modal similarities and add a regularizer that increases path sharpness and monotonicity while allowing small warps.

Architecturally, SAMM uses a single fusion transformer with relative position encodings for time and attention biases for graph topology. Lightweight per-modality heads share the backbone and are linked by cross-attention during decoding so that predictions for one stream can directly condition on hidden states from another when it is unmasked. Training follows a curriculum: begin with shorter spans, lower mask ratios, and mostly random masks; ramp up to higher ratios with larger contiguous blocks, more structure-aware subgraph masks, and more frequent modality dropouts. A compute-aware data scheduler mixes datasets and sensor pools based on learned utility and repetition half-lives, allocating training steps where they yield the greatest marginal improvement. At finetuning time, we freeze most of the backbone and attach small task heads (forecasting, anomaly detection, link prediction, node classification, imputation), optionally continuing masked objectives at low weight for stability.

In deployment, SAMM enables: imputation under missing sensors by reconstructing masked modalities; transfer across domains via shared discrete codebooks and alignment; and robust downstream performance with few labels due to structure- and importance-aware pretraining. Ablations vary tokenization granularity, mask composition, discretization vs pure regression, alignment regularization, and compute-aware scheduling to characterize what matters most per domain.

### Rationale
Masked prediction works when tokens, masks, and targets reflect the data’s structure and uncertainty. For streams, contiguity, channel correlations, and time–frequency duality matter; for graphs, masking should respect topology and community structure. Importance-aware masking focuses learning capacity on informative regions while retaining coverage of background context, echoing how masked language modeling benefits from content words but still sees function words. Discrete reconstruction targets stabilize training for continuous signals and avoid blurry averages; residual heads keep precision. Cross-modal contrastive and matching losses encourage the model to use complementary sensors rather than treating them as noise, and a soft alignment-path regularizer makes masked prediction feasible under time warps without requiring exact frame alignment.

The compute-aware data scheduler acknowledges that heterogeneous data pools contribute unequally and saturate with repetition, so it allocates pretraining steps and mask types where they are most valuable. Together, these choices adapt the masked-prediction principle beyond text and images by aligning the masking unit to structure (spans, subgraphs, channels), the prediction target to noise and ambiguity (discrete codes plus residuals), and the objective to cross-sensor alignment and discrimination.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [MULTIMODAL] ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision (2102.03334)
   problem: Vision-and-Language Pre-training models typically rely on heavy visual embedders (region detectors or deep CNN backbones), making inference slow and constraining expressiveness to a predefined visual vocabulary.
   method:  ViLT replaces region/CNN visual features with a shallow, convolution-free linear patch projection and uses a single-stream transformer to jointly process image patches and text tokens. The transformer is initialized from ViT-B/32 pretrained on ImageNet, with image patches (P=32) and text embeddings concatenated with type embeddings and fed through 12 transformer layers. Pretrained with ITM and MLM, this monolithic design yields large efficiency gains (e.g., ~15 ms vs. ~900 ms for region-feature models; ≥4× faster than grid-feature models) while achieving competitive VQAv2, NLVR2, and retrieval performance.

2. [MULTIMODAL] Scaling Laws for Data Filtering: Data Curation cannot be Compute Agnostic (2404.07177)
   problem: Data filtering for vision-language model pretraining is typically compute-agnostic and existing scaling laws treat web data as homogeneous. This fails to capture that high-quality data is limited and loses utility with repetition, and offers no way to predict performance for mixtures of different-quality pools without costly joint training.
   method:  Introduce compute-aware scaling laws tailored to heterogeneous web data. Each data pool is parameterized by a utility exponent b and a repetition half-life τ, modeling exponential decay in utility per repetition (δ = (1/2)^(1/τ)) and yielding a closed-form for repeated epochs. For mixtures, prove Theorem 1: the effective utility b_eff at repetition k is a weighted mean of per-pool utilities and the half-life scales linearly with the number of mixed pools (τ̂ = p·τ); in contrastive CLIP training, account for O(N^2) comparisons so τ̂ scales with total pool size (τ̂ = (N̂/N)·τ). Using these, estimate performance for any pool combination and compute budget without ever training on the combinations.

3. [MULTIMODAL] HyperPELT: Unified Parameter-Efficient Language Model Tuning for Both Language and Vision-and-Language Tasks (2203.03878)
   problem: Equipping text-pretrained LMs with vision-and-language capabilities efficiently—without appending image tokens that consume input length or full-model fine-tuning—requires a new mechanism to inject visual information with few parameters.
   method:  VL-HyperPELT treats image features as inputs to hypernetworks rather than as sequence tokens: CLIP-extracted visual features are projected into visual hyper-embeddings, which drive visual hypernetworks to generate visual-specific prefix vectors for attention and adapter weights for FFN. These are combined with task-specific outputs (concatenating prefix vectors; parallel adapters) to modulate the frozen T5 while preserving the original text input length. This approach achieves comparable or better performance than VL-Adapter with fewer trainable parameters (6.62% vs 7.16%) and yields stronger few-shot transfer on OKVQA and SNLI-VE.

4. [MULTIMODAL] Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training (2210.08773)
   problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
   method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

5. [MULTIMODAL] ViLT: Vision-and-Language Transformer Without Convolution or Region Supervision (2102.03334)
   problem: Vision-and-Language Pre-training models typically rely on heavy visual embedders (region detectors or deep CNN backbones), making inference slow and constraining expressiveness to a predefined visual vocabulary.
   method:  ViLT replaces region/CNN visual features with a shallow, convolution-free linear patch projection and uses a single-stream transformer to jointly process image patches and text tokens. The transformer is initialized from ViT-B/32 pretrained on ImageNet, with image patches (P=32) and text embeddings concatenated with type embeddings and fed through 12 transformer layers. Pretrained with ITM and MLM, this monolithic design yields large efficiency gains (e.g., ~15 ms vs. ~900 ms for region-feature models; ≥4× faster than grid-feature models) while achieving competitive VQAv2, NLVR2, and retrieval performance.

6. [MULTIMODAL] Unicoder-VL: A Universal Encoder for Vision and Language by Cross-modal Pre-training (1908.06066)
   problem: Existing CV and NLP pretraining fail to jointly model fine-grained interactions between image regions and long natural-language sequences, limiting performance on cross-modal tasks like retrieval and visual commonsense reasoning.
   method:  Unicoder-VL is a single-stream multi-layer Transformer (initialized from BERT) that ingests wordpieces together with region-level visual features and 5-D box geometry from a frozen Faster R-CNN (top-100 RoIs), projecting visual and location features into a shared space with layer normalization. It is cross-modally pre-trained on 3.8M image–caption pairs with three tasks: (i) Masked Language Modeling masking 15% of words and predicting them conditioned on all image regions; (ii) Masked Object Classification masking 15% of regions (90% replaced with zero vectors) and predicting detector-derived object labels from cross-modal context; (iii) Visual–Linguistic Matching using the [CLS] representation to classify matched vs. mismatched pairs with both negative-image and negative-caption sampling. This yields context-aware, aligned token and region embeddings that fuse vision and language early.

7. [CV] Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion (2012.02107)
   problem: Per-object, per-pixel feed-forward segmentations often conflict in overlap regions (multiple objects claiming the same pixels), especially under heavy or same-category occlusions, leading to erroneous masks and classifications.
   method:  Introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module (ORM) that operates on the per-object likelihood maps. It detects conflicts where two objects both predict foreground, resolves them via pixel-level competition by assigning each contested pixel to the object with higher foreground likelihood, and then recovers the pairwise occlusion order by comparing the number of reassigned pixels in the conflict set. Using this order, it enforces consistent labeling in overlaps and feeds the corrected occlusion priors back into the CompositionalNet to mask occluded features and recompute class likelihoods, iterating this top-down self-correction to improve both segmentation and classification under occlusion.

8. [MULTIMODAL] OpenIns3D: Snap and Lookup for 3D Open-vocabulary Instance Segmentation (2309.00616)
   problem: 3D open-vocabulary scene understanding without aligned images suffers from excessive, low-quality instance masks when using class-agnostic proposals, hurting downstream recognition.
   method:  Introduce a class-agnostic Mask Proposal Module (built from Mask3D with all classification heads and label-dependent components removed) augmented with (i) Mask Scoring that predicts each mask’s IoU via a shallow MLP trained with L2 loss and a γ-weight to down-weight unmatched masks, and (ii) Mask Filtering that keeps only masks above a learned IoU threshold β, enforces stability via dual-threshold consistency with offset α (IoU > 80%), and removes tiny masks below N_min points. These choices sharply reduce noisy proposals and improve mask quality, as verified by AP gains in ablations.

9. [CV] Prediction-Guided Distillation for Dense Object Detection (2203.05469)
   problem: Feature imitation–based knowledge distillation for dense object detectors underperforms because it distills large foreground regions using hand-crafted, GT-dependent masks that ignore where the teacher actually makes its best predictions.
   method:  Prediction-Guided Distillation (PGD) introduces a Prediction-Guided Weighting (PGW) module that scores teacher predictions per location using a quality metric combining classification probability and IoU (per [8]). It selects the top-K highest-quality locations across all FPN levels as key predictive regions and fits an adaptive 2D Gaussian via MLE over these points to produce a smooth, parameterless foreground weighting mask. Distillation losses are then focused on these regions, emphasizing the teacher’s most accurate predictions rather than all GT-covered pixels. This teacher-driven, adaptive weighting outperforms center/box-based or raw quality-weighting strategies.

10. [MULTIMODAL] BabyWalk: Going Farther in Vision-and-Language Navigation by Taking Baby Steps (2005.04625)
   problem: Direct reinforcement learning on long-horizon VLN instructions is unstable, and imitation learning on whole trajectories does not adequately prepare agents for long, uncertain executions.
   method:  Use a two-phase training pipeline: (1) micro-level imitation learning where each BABY-STEP is learned independently with the agent 'preset' in expert context to match expert sub-trajectories; (2) curriculum-based reinforcement learning that progressively increases the number of consecutive BABY-STEPs the agent must execute (lectures), providing expert history up to the start and optimizing a fidelity-oriented reward (CLS) via policy gradients. This curriculum (up to 4 lectures) consistently improves over direct RL on full instructions, with stepwise gains in SDTW and CLS.

11. [RL] Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL (2406.01939)
   problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
   method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).

12. [ROBOTICS] Think Global, Act Local: Dual-scale Graph Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation (2202.11742)
   problem: Behavior cloning for sequential policies suffers from train-test distribution shift, while reinforcement learning in VLN is difficult due to sparse rewards.
   method:  DUET is first pretrained with behavior cloning and auxiliary cross-modal tasks (masked language modeling, masked region classification, single-step action prediction, and object grounding) to initialize grounded representations. It is then fine-tuned with a pseudo interactive demonstrator (PID) akin to DAgger: during rollouts, a demonstrator with access to the full environment graph selects pseudo-target actions as navigable nodes minimizing the combined distance from the current node to the node and from the node to the goal. The policy is optimized with a balance between expert and pseudo labels, yielding stronger navigation performance than RL-based fine-tuning in experiments.

   problem: Generative self-supervision on KGs often relies on random masking, which fails to highlight task-relevant knowledge and can be sensitive to KG noise.
   method:  KGRec introduces a rationale-aware masked autoencoder: it derives globally comparable importance γ(h,r,t) by scaling softmax rationale scores with the head’s degree and adds Gumbel noise for robust top‑k selection. High‑γ triplets are masked to form an augmented KG, embeddings are computed on the remaining graph, and a relation-aware dot‑product decoder reconstructs the masked edges. This masking‑reconstruction forces the model to rebuild crucial connections and distill informative KG rationales for recommendation.

On BEIR, LLM-based feedback improves recall@100 over cross-encoder feedback (e.g., 71.2% vs 69.0% average), and combining both signals yields further gains (72.0%).

A novel cross-attention link allows the DNC decoder to attend directly to the ASR decoder’s token-level hidden features (W_CA), aligning predicted speaker indices with the serialized word tokens and enabling joint optimization under a single loss. They propose the Alignment Path Score (APS), which aggregates alignment-path similarity across tokens and layers to quantify fine-grained alignment quality. APS exhibits stronger linear correlation with the modality gap than sequence-level metrics (e.g., higher R^2 under LoRA), and alignment paths show high monotonicity and consistency across models (e.g., Spearman ≈ 0.85 for cosine), indicating token-level alignment is a key mechanism underlying LSLM speech understanding.

   method:  FuSe finetunes pre-trained generalist policies by using natural language as a common cross-modal ground. Two auxiliary language-grounding losses are introduced alongside the behavior cloning loss: (1) a CLIP-style multimodal contrastive loss that builds an observation embedding (via a multi-head attention fusion over modalities) and aligns it with every instruction formed from available modality annotations; (2) a multimodal generative language loss that uses a single transformer head with modality tokens to generate the appropriate instruction from each modality combination. The final objective is L = L_BC + β L_gen + λ L_contrast, and instruction space is broadened via LLM rephrasing of templated annotations. This language-grounded finetuning unlocks zero-shot multimodal prompting, cross-modal prompting, and compositional reasoning, and applies across architectures (Octo and a 3B PaliGemma-based VLA), improving success rates by over 20% vs. baselines.

20. [ROBOTICS] Hearing Touch: Audio-Visual Pretraining for Contact-Rich Manipulation (2405.08576)
   problem: Robotic manipulation in low-data regimes benefits from tactile sensing, but large-scale pretraining exists mainly for vision. Collecting internet-scale tactile datasets is impractical, and vision alone struggles to perceive subtle contact events, limiting generalization.
   method:  Use piezo contact microphones on the robot gripper to capture contact-rich vibrations as audio and transfer internet-scale audio-visual pretraining to this modality. Train an end-to-end behavior cloning policy that fuses audio and vision via self-attention to predict actions. This is, to the authors’ knowledge, the first use of large-scale multisensory (audio-visual) pretraining for manipulation and yields substantial gains over vision-only and audio-from-scratch/BYOL-A baselines across three real tasks, with improved robustness to large visual domain shifts.

21. [ROBOTICS] Grasp Anything: Combining Teacher-Augmented Policy Gradient Learning with Instance Segmentation to Grasp Arbitrary Objects (2403.10187)
   problem: Learning dexterous grasping directly from visual inputs is sample-inefficient, and naïve policy distillation from a privileged teacher fails to adapt when the student's visual observation space changes the feasible optimal behavior (e.g., self-occlusion breaks tracking).
   method:  Teacher-Augmented Policy Gradient (TAPG): a two-stage student–teacher framework. First, train a teacher with policy gradients on privileged low-dimensional states (oriented 3D bounding boxes). Then train a visual student on segmented point clouds with a combined objective: standard policy gradient plus a behavior cloning term gated by the teacher’s advantage/value, so the student imitates only when the teacher is estimated better. This permits deviation to handle visual constraints and comes with a performance guarantee under bounded imitation loss. TAPG learns perception-aware strategies and outperforms both visual RL and pure policy distillation, surpassing the teacher after adaptation.

4. LLM-Integrated Bayesian State Space Models for Multimodal Time-Series Forecasting (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 2)
   Forecasting in the real world requires integrating structured time-series data with unstructured textual information, but existing methods are architecturally limited by fixed input/output horizons and are unable to model or quantify uncertainty. We address this challenge by introducing LLM-integrated Bayesian State space models (LBS), a novel probabilistic framework for multimodal temporal forecasting. At a high level, LBS consists of two components: (1) a state space model (SSM) backbone that captures the temporal dynamics of latent states from which both numerical and textual observations are generated and (2) a pretrained large language model (LLM) that is adapted to encode textual inputs for posterior state estimation and decode textual forecasts consistent with the latent trajectory. This design enables flexible lookback and forecast windows, principled uncertainty quantification, and improved temporal generalization thanks to the well-suited inductive bias of SSMs toward modeling dynamical systems. Experiments on the TextTimeCorpus benchmark demonstrate that LBS improves the previous state-of-the-art by 13.20% while providing human-readable summaries of each forecast. Our work is the first to unify LLMs and SSMs for joint numerical and textual prediction, offering a novel foundation for multimodal temporal reasoning.

This method significantly enhances the model's ability to integrate multimodal information, leading to notable improvements in classification, prediction, and anomaly detection tasks. The resultant embeddings are pivotal for downstream tasks like clustering and visualization, encapsulating the interactions within multimodal time series data effectively. Furthermore, this work incorporates transfer learning with pretrained transformers to harness extensive knowledge from large datasets, adapting it to multimodal time series analysis. This strategy excels in capturing long-range dependencies, thereby augmenting generalization and performance in data-scarce scenarios. 7. Automatic detection and prediction of epileptic EEG signals based on nonlinear dynamics and deep learning: a review (Frontiers in Neuroscience 2025, cites: 16)
   Epilepsy is a neurological disorder affecting ~50 million patients worldwide (30% refractory cases) with complex dynamical behavior governed by nonlinear differential equations. Seizures severely impact patients' quality of life and may lead to serious complications. As a primary diagnostic tool, electroencephalography (EEG) captures brain dynamics through non-stationary time series with measurable chaotic and fractal properties. However, EEG signals are highly nonlinear and non-smooth, and conventional linear analysis methods limited by Fourier spectral decomposition cannot capture the inherent phase space dispersion and multifractal geometries of epileptic signals. In recent years, nonlinear dynamics methods such as chaos theory, fractal analysis, and entropy computation have provided new perspectives for EEG signal analysis, while deep learning approaches like convolutional neural networks and long short-term memory networks further enhance the robustness of dynamical pattern recognition through end-to-end nonlinear feature extraction. These methods reveal dynamic patterns in signals, thereby substantially improving epilepsy detection and prediction accuracy. This survey reviews research progress in automatic detection and prediction of epileptic EEG signals based on nonlinear dynamics and deep learning, evaluating key techniques including Lyapunov exponents, fractal dimensions, and entropy metrics. Results highlight three paradigm shifts, including the demonstrated superiority of nonlinear features in capturing preictal transitions, the critical role of attention mechanisms in processing long-range dependencies, and the significant advantages achieved by integrating nonlinear attributes with deep learning architectures for cross-patient generalization and noise suppression. Furthermore, this survey identifies persistent challenges including clinical translation barriers, algorithm performance trade-offs, and feature extraction/selection limitations. It emphasizes the need to integrate algebraic topology and graph convolutional deep learning to address multiscale dynamics, and proposes a unified framework for regulatory-compliant clinical translation that bridges the gap between research innovations and real-world clinical deployment, while outlining future research priorities focused on multimodal data fusion and regulatory-compliant validation frameworks.

   With the increasing demand for high-quality telecommunication services, cellular KPI prediction becomes crucial for telecommunication network monitoring and management. In this work, we propose a novel framework for cellular KPI prediction, which considers its distribution discrepancy under different network operation scenarios. In particular, three specific predictors for normal, target alarm, and neighbor alarm scenarios are proposed based on spatiotemporal graph neural networks and unified through transfer learning. Temporal convolution and attention mechanism are embedded to model the impact of anomalies on KPIs and its propagation across neighboring cells according to the cellular network topology. An experiment on a real cellular KPI dataset shows the effectiveness of the proposed method compared to the state-of-the-arts. Note to Practitioners—Cellular network KPI prediction under scenarios of network alarms is crucial to evaluate the impact of alarms on network services and guides cellular network maintenance policies. This problem is similar to a general multivariate time series prediction problem with data multimodality. However, the first challenge in our case is that, under different scenarios, i.e., normal, target alarm, and neighbor alarm, the effective information and spatiotemporal dependencies among KPIs are different. The second challenge is the imbalanced or sparse sample size for specific scenarios, deteriorating the model performance. This paper proposes a cellular KPI prediction framework consisting of three scenario-specific predictors with similar but different modules to process different scenario-specific data. To address the dataset imbalance across scenarios, we adopt a transfer learning strategy to unify the training and prediction of three predictors. The experiment results on a real cellular KPI dataset demonstrate that the proposed framework is more feasible and effective than the state-of-the-art models for multivariate time series prediction. Future research can consider developing maintenance policies such that the cost caused by abnormal KPIs can be minimized.

9. LSTTN: A Long-Short Term Transformer-based Spatio-temporal Neural Network for Traffic Flow Forecasting (Knowledge-Based Systems 2024, cites: 99)
   Accurate traffic forecasting is a fundamental problem in intelligent transportation systems and learning long-range traffic representations with key information through spatiotemporal graph neural networks (STGNNs) is a basic assumption of current traffic flow prediction models. However, due to structural limitations, existing STGNNs can only utilize short-range traffic flow data; therefore, the models cannot adequately learn the complex trends and periodic features in traffic flow. Besides, it is challenging to extract the key temporal information from the long historical traffic series and obtain a compact representation. To solve the above problems, we propose a novel LSTTN (Long-Short Term Transformer-based Network) framework comprehensively considering the long- and short-term features in historical traffic flow. First, we employ a masked subseries Transformer to infer the content of masked subseries from a small portion of unmasked subseries and their temporal context in a pretraining manner, forcing the model to efficiently learn compressed and contextual subseries temporal representations from long historical series. Then, based on the learned representations, long-term trend is extracted by using stacked 1D dilated convolution layers, and periodic features are extracted by dynamic graph convolution layers. For the difficulties in making time-step level prediction, LSTTN adopts a short-term trend extractor to learn fine-grained short-term temporal features. Finally, LSTTN fuses the long-term trend, periodic features and short-term features to obtain the prediction results. Experiments on four real-world datasets show that in 60-minute-ahead long-term forecasting, the LSTTN model achieves a minimum improvement of 5.63\% and a maximum improvement of 16.78\% over baseline models. The source code is available at https://github.com/GeoX-Lab/LSTTN.

1. Enhancing LLMs for Time Series Forecasting via Structure-Guided Cross-Modal Alignment (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 2)
   The emerging paradigm of leveraging pretrained large language models (LLMs) for time series forecasting has predominantly employed linguistic-temporal modality alignment strategies through token-level or layer-wise feature mapping. However, these approaches fundamentally neglect a critical insight: the core competency of LLMs resides not merely in processing localized token features but in their inherent capacity to model holistic sequence structures. This paper posits that effective cross-modal alignment necessitates structural consistency at the sequence level. We propose the Structure-Guided Cross-Modal Alignment (SGCMA), a framework that fully exploits and aligns the state-transition graph structures shared by time-series and linguistic data as sequential modalities, thereby endowing time series with language-like properties and delivering stronger generalization after modality alignment. SGCMA consists of two key components, namely Structure Alignment and Semantic Alignment. In Structure Alignment, a state transition matrix is learned from text data through Hidden Markov Models (HMMs), and a shallow transformer-based Maximum Entropy Markov Model (MEMM) receives the hot-start transition matrix and annotates each temporal patch into state probability, ensuring that the temporal representation sequence inherits language-like sequential dynamics. In Semantic Alignment, cross-attention is applied between temporal patches and the top-k tokens within each state, and the ultimate temporal embeddings are derived by the expected value of these embeddings using a weighted average based on state probabilities. Experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that SGCMA achieves state-of-the-art performance, offering a novel approach to cross-modal alignment in time series forecasting.

The model encodes image, text, transaction time series, and blockchain behavioral features, constructs a heterogeneous NFT interaction graph (co-transaction, shared creator, wallet relation, and price co-movement), and jointly performs relation-aware graph attention and global temporal–structural transformer reasoning with an adaptive fusion gate. Specifically, it employs a Graphormer encoder to model inter-lead dependencies in ECG signals and a Vision Transformer to extract morphological patterns from ECG images, both modulated by patient-specific laboratory features using Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM). A Transformer-based fusion module captures cross-modal interactions, while a contrastive learning objective encourages alignment between signal and image modalities. Experimental results on a real-world clinical dataset and three public benchmarks demonstrate that GFM-MIP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across multiple evaluation metrics. Ablation studies further validate the contribution of each modality and architectural component. The proposed framework offers a clinically meaningful and scalable solution for robust, multimodal cardiovascular diagnosis.

The proposed approach utilizes modality specialized tokenizers, a shared transformer architecture, and cross-attention mechanisms to project the data from different modalities into a unified embedding space. It addresses multimodal and multitask scenarios by incorpo-rating modality-specific task heads for different tasks in respective modalities. We propose a novel pretraining strategy with iterative modality switching to initialize the network, and a training algorithm which trades off fully joint training over all modalities, with training on pairs of modalities at a time. We provide comprehensive evaluation across 25 datasets from 12 modalities and show state of the art performances, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed architecture, pretraining strategy and adapted multitask training.

HyperModal integrates a dual-space contrastive objective that aligns within-modality consistency and cross-modality co-occurrence, enabling the model to learn modality-agnostic but semantically grounded representations. However, heterogeneity across devices and frequent sensor dropout pose significant challenges for unified modelling of these multimodal signals. We present \texttt{sleep2vec}, a foundation model for diverse and incomplete nocturnal biosignals that learns a shared representation via cross-modal alignment. \texttt{sleep2vec} is contrastively pre-trained on 42,249 overnight recordings spanning nine modalities using a \textit{Demography, Age, Site \&History-aware InfoNCE} objective that incorporates physiological and acquisition metadata (\textit{e.g.}, age, gender, recording site) to dynamically weight negatives and mitigate cohort-specific shortcuts. On downstream sleep staging and clinical outcome assessment, \texttt{sleep2vec} consistently outperforms strong baselines and remains robust to any subset of available modalities and sensor dropout. We further characterize, to our knowledge for the first time, scaling laws for nocturnal biosignals with respect to modality diversity and model capacity. Together, these results show that unified cross-modal alignment, coupled with principled scaling, enables label-efficient, general-purpose modelling of real-world nocturnal biosignals.

Conventional fusion methods-such as wavelet-, Laplacian-, and decision-level approaches-often fail to preserve spatial correspondence across modalities and suffer from annotation of inconsistencies, limiting their robustness in real-world settings. This study introduces two fusion strategies, Registration-aware Guided Image Fusion (RGIF) and Reliability-Gated Modality-Attention Fusion (RGMAF), designed to overcome these limitations. RGIF employs Enhanced Correlation Coefficient (ECC)-based affine registration combined with guided filtering to maintain thermal saliency while enhancing structural detail. RGMAF integrates affine and optical-flow registration with a reliability-weighted attention mechanism that adaptively balances thermal contrast and visual sharpness. Experiments were conducted on the Multi-Sensor and Multi-View Fixed-Wing (MMFW)-UAV dataset comprising 147,417 annotated air-to-air frames collected from infrared, wide-angle, and zoom sensors. Among single-modality detectors, YOLOv10x demonstrated the most stable cross-domain performance and was selected as the detection backbone for evaluating fused imagery. RGIF improved the visual baseline by 2.13% mAP@50 (achieving 97.65%), while RGMAF attained the highest recall of 98.64%. These findings show that registration-aware and reliability-adaptive fusion provides a robust framework for integrating heterogeneous modalities, substantially enhancing UAV detection performance in multimodal environments.

   Human action recognition (HAR) with multi-modal inputs (RGB-D, skeleton, point cloud) can achieve high accuracy but typically relies on large labeled datasets and degrades sharply when sensors fail or are noisy. RCMCL jointly optimizes (i) a cross-modal contrastive objective that aligns heterogeneous streams, (ii) an intra-modal self-distillation objective that improves view-invariance and reduces redundancy, and (iii) a degradation simulation objective that explicitly trains models to recover from masked or corrupted inputs. At inference, an Adaptive Modality Gating (AMG) network assigns data-driven reliability weights to each modality for robust fusion. On NTU RGB+D 120 (CS/CV) and UWA3D-II, RCMCL attains state-of-the-art accuracy in standard settings and exhibits markedly better robustness: under severe dual-modality dropout it shows only an 11.5% degradation, significantly outperforming strong supervised fusion baselines. We propose PhysioBridge, a foundation-model approach that learns a shared representation space for ECG and PPG via self-supervised pretraining and explicit physiology constraints, then supports downstream adaptation with distribution-free risk control. PhysioBridge introduces (i) multi-rate patch tokenization that preserves clinically meaningful morphology across heterogeneous sampling rates; (ii) coupled masked modeling and cross-modal contrastive alignment to bind ECG and PPG dynamics while remaining label-efficient; (iii) differentiable physiology regularizers that enforce heart-rate consistency and pulse-transit-time plausibility; and (iv) calibrated conformal prediction wrappers that provide coverage guarantees for classification and regression under minimal assumptions. To leverage this dataset, we introduce GeoMeld-FM, a pretraining framework that combines multi-pretext masked autoencoding over aligned modalities, JEPA representation learning, and caption-vision contrastive alignment. This joint objective enables the learned representation space to capture both reliable cross-sensor physical consistency and grounded semantics. Experiments demonstrate consistent gains in downstream transfer and cross-sensor robustness. Together, GeoMeld and GeoMeld-FM establish a scalable reference framework for semantically grounded multi-modal foundation modeling in remote sensing.

To obtain sensor-agnostic representations, we train a single, unified transformer model reconstructing masked multi-sensor data with cross-sensor token mixup. On both single- and multi-modal tasks across diverse sensors, SMARTIES outperforms previous models that rely on sensor-specific pretraining. Our code and pretrained models are available at https://gsumbul.github.io/SMARTIES.

   Many healthcare applications are inherently multimodal, involving several physiological signals. This is partly due to challenges in multimodal health data: obtaining data across many patients is difficult and costly, there is a lot of inter-subject variability, and modalities are often heterogeneously informative across downstream tasks. We show that the model learns representations that can be linearly probed for a diverse set of downstream tasks. We also find that late-fusion models pretrained with contrastive learning objectives are less effective across multiple tasks. Finally, we analyze the model's representations, showing that attention weights become more cross-modal and temporally aligned with our pretraining strategy. The learned embeddings also become more distributed in terms of the modalities encoded by each unit. Overall, our work demonstrates the utility of multimodal foundation models with health data, even across diverse physiological data sources. We further argue that explicit methods for inducing cross-modality may enhance multimodal pretraining strategies.

This paper explores the design and development of multimodal learning architectures that effectively fuse features from diverse modalities through early, late, and hybrid fusion strategies, along with attention-based mechanisms and crossmodal transformers. Recent advancements, including contrastive learning and large-scale pretraining on multimodal datasets, have significantly improved the performance of applications such as visual question answering, speech recognition, sentiment analysis, and autonomous systems. The proposed framework emphasizes modality alignment, feature representation learning, and robust fusion techniques to address challenges such as data heterogeneity, modality imbalance, and missing modalities. Moreover, the role of selfsupervised learning and generative models, including multimodal large language models (MLLMs), is discussed in enhancing cross-modal understanding and zero-shot generalization. We highlight benchmark datasets, evaluation metrics, and case studies that demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed approaches. Finally, open challenges such as interpretability, computational efficiency, and ethical considerations are outlined, with future research directions aimed at building scalable, generalizable, and human-centric multimodal systems.

This paper introduces the vision of AviationLMM, a Large Multimodal foundation Model for civil aviation, designed to unify the heterogeneous data streams of civil aviation and enable understanding, reasoning, generation and agentic applications. By articulating the design and challenges of AviationLMM, we aim to boost the civil aviation foundation model progress and catalyze coordinated research efforts toward an integrated, trustworthy and privacy - preserving aviation AI ecosystem.

   Generative self-supervised learning (SSL), especially masked autoencoders, has become one of the most exciting learning paradigms and has shown great potential in handling graph data. However, real-world graphs are always heterogeneous, which poses three critical challenges that existing methods ignore: 1) how to capture complex graph structure? 2) how to incorporate various node attributes? and 3) how to encode different node positions? In light of this, we study the problem of generative SSL on heterogeneous graphs and propose HGMAE, a novel heterogeneous graph masked autoencoder model to address these challenges. HGMAE captures comprehensive graph information via two innovative masking techniques and three unique training strategies. In particular, we first develop metapath masking and adaptive attribute masking with dynamic mask rate to enable effective and stable learning on heterogeneous graphs. We then design several training strategies including metapath-based edge reconstruction to adopt complex structural information, target attribute restoration to incorporate various node attributes, and positional feature prediction to encode node positional information. To address this limitation, we propose MaskPOI, a novel self-supervised learning framework that combines the strengths of graph neural networks and masked modeling. MaskPOI incorporates two complementary modules: an edge mask-based graph autoencoder that models the spatial topology by predicting edge existence and uncovering hidden spatial relationships and a feature mask-based graph autoencoder that reconstructs masked node features to explore the rich attribute characteristics of POIs.
